Dear Commission Members: 
On February 9, 1977 Mr. D. J. Callaghan transmitted to your 
Commission the proposed Conflict of Interest Code adopted by 
the District's Board of Directors on January 19, 1977. 
On February 10, 1977 I wrote your Commission concerning the 
proposed Code. 
Both letters detailed the concern raised at 
the Board's hearing relative to the inclusion of the Advisory 
Council as designated employees. 
At the February 16, 1977 meeting of the District's Board of 
Directors Mrs. Ruth Church Gupta, chairperson of the Advisory 
Council, addressed the Board with respect to the Board's 
inclusion of the members of the Advisory Council as "designated 
employees." 
Mrs. Gupta reviewed for the Board the report 
"Action Taken by Advisory Council Executive Committee with 
Respect to Inclusion in Conflict of Interest Code Adopted by 
the Board of Directors Janu~ry 19, 1977" which urged the 
Board to withdraw the inclusion of the Advisory Council 
from the Conflict of Int~rest Code. 
A copy of this report 
is attached. 
Mrs. Gupta also reviewed various aspects of the law relating 
to "designated employees" with the Board and discussed advice 
recently given to the State Board of Control by the legal 
counsel to State Controller Cory. 
She indicated that the 
counsel to the Controller had expressed the feeling that 
'dvisory Council members should not be included as "designated 
employees." 
She also reviewed the statutory provision which 
requires that members of the Advisory Council must come from 
specific disciplines. 
In its discussion of this matter, the Board adopted a resolution 
asking me to meet with the Advisory Council to discuss with 
them the possibility of amending the Code to limit the dis-
closure made by Council members. 
Members of the Council would 
be put in a category such that less than all financial interests 
would have to be reported. 
It was suggested that they might 
be limited in their disclosure to interests in regulated 
industries.

to discuss possible modifications of the scope of disclosure required 
of members of the Council. 
Such a question of possible modification, 
however, will be an issue for consideration by. the Board of Direc-
tors only in the event that your Commission concludes that members 
of the Advisory (:i)(Hl(:::il

employees." 
Pertinent portions of the minutes of the Board of Directors 
meeting of February 16, 1977 are enclosed. 
Please advise if I may be of any assistance in this matter.

The Executive Corn .... ni ttee to submi t to the Board of Directors 
the following report with respect to the Board's adopted 
Conflict of Interest Code: 
That the Executive Committee takes exception to the 
Board's inclusion of the Advisory Council for the 
following reasons: 
(1) 
It is clear that it Has not the intent for advisory 
Boards, t'lho are not compensn. ted and not employees, 
to be included under the provisions of the Code, 
as per §820l9 of the Government Code, as follo\,/s: 
"Designated employee" does not include an 
elected state officer or any unsalaried member 
of any board or com:nission \-,hich serves a sole-
ly advisory function." 
'(2) 'That it appears ~o be in direct conflict with the 
enabling legislation setting up the Advisory CounCil; 
(3) 
That such an inclusion Nas decided without a 
hearing of the Advisory Council; 
and furthermore, 
That the Executive COlmnittee understands from the 
Minutes of ttle Doard meeting that the motivation for 
including the Advisor:: Council is for the purpose of 
improvine the imaGe of the District. If the latter 
is correct, it seems that it \'lOuld be appropriate to 
refer ~uch a matter to the Advisory Council for 
suggestions as to hON the image of the District might 
be improved and C0l11I11ent on any specific ideas that the 
Board f,lD.y be contemplatinG in this respect;· 
1'ha t the Executi ve COir~mi t tee would like to point out 
the inaccurLlcy in the Board Hinutes of January 19th 
that Advisory Council r:lCmbcrs were advised during their 
personal interview of the Board's intention to act with 
respect to conflict of interest or disclosure; 
That the point that "since many of its (Council's) 
recol11I1lCndations are adopted by the Board" the Council 
does in effect mLlke decisions is invalid. 
According to 
this vlew, the only Advisory Council referred to in the 
exclusions of FPPC would be an advi~ory body whose 
recommend.:;. tions are not in anYYlay adopted. 


" . 
r 
Therefore, the Corr'..!ni ttee urges the Board to "Ii tlidraw 
the inclusion of the Advisory Council from the Code.

APPROVAL OF !<1I!'ruTES OF FEBRUARY 2, 1977 
On motion of Mr. Tamaras; seconded by Mr. Kepny; carried • 
REPORT OF THE ADVISORY COur;cIL - CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE 
Ruth Church Gupta, Chairperson 
Mrs. Gupta revie~red the report, Action Taken by Advisory Council 
Executive Committee '~'lith Respect to Inclusion in ConOict of Intere~ 
Code adopted by the Board of Directors February 4, 1977, urging the 
Board to ~.fi thdrn.t'l the inclusion of the Advisory Council from the 
Code. 
She advised that the Executive Co~~ittee did discuss the 
disqualific8.tion portion of the Code \'li th respect to service on the 
Council, pointing out that the 10.\'1 specifically requires Council 
members to be from specific disc!plines; that the legal counsel 
to State Controller Cory advised the St~te Board of Control that 
the FPPC intends to be more exclusive than inclusive in the. Designat{ 
Employee area and that it was his feeling that Advisory Council 
members are not included in said desit~ation; that the Executive 
Committee did poll the members inquiring about the question of 
conflict of interest being asked ~'lhcn intervier;red, and that although 
some members could not recn.ll, the feeling generally \1aS that they 
were asked \':hether they i'~ere involved in any activity ~'lhich might 
create a conflict of interest to which the response was that they 
saw no problem; that the Executive Co:;",;nittee did discuss the possi-
bili ty of adopting a program that ~'lould sta te the Council's philosopy 
that anyone having a conflict would disclose it without going into 
the reporting requirements of a lot of paperwork which would not 
accomplish the purpose of the legislation. 
In discussion, ~frs. Guota stated that the Council members were 
polled specifically as-to the follm'ling questions: 
Was the subject of conflict of interest raised, and an answer 
requested; 
Was a willingness to disclose asked, and an answer given, 
during their intervie\'l with the Personnel Committee and the answers 
generally t'lere "no". 
Mrs. Koehler advised that she had requested Ms. Harder to check the 
tape of the Personnel Con~mittee meeting of November 17, 1976, when 
the list of questions asked of the Applicants Nas reviewed and 
conflict of interest \o{as not among them. 
She stated that during 
the interviews the Personnel COlmni ttee became D.r;mrp. that many 
consultants had applied to be on the CoUt."1cil, and that the question 
of conflict of interest was asked of some of the applicants because 
the COlT'.mittee was concerned they want:::;d to serve fo-:: their own gain, 
but that the question war; not asl\.cd of all Advisory Council candidates.
She apolOGized for any confusion in this area.

, . 
Ji 
Mr. Cooper stated that Alameda County Counsel advised that it 
might be possible to adopt a Code requiring filing of income and 
~vestments in reGUlated industries and suc;gestcd the Counci~ 
discuss this approach illi th the LeGal Counsel. 
After further discussion, Hr. Cooper moved to instruct the Counsel 
to meet ~'dth the Advisory Council in an attempt to develop a more 
restrictive list of ~ihat particular interests need to be divuJ.ged 
by members of the Advisory Council and report back to the Board 
as to how it mi3ht restrict it to those areas where there are 
conflicts; seconded.by Mr. Tamaras • 
. 
In discussion, Hr. Bacciocco pointed out that the Board wanted to 
make sure that anyone serving on the Council did not profit indirect 
or directly by a collusive relationship with a source which appears 
before the Board and that if the Council keeps that in mind every 
member of the pub~ic I'lill be protected. 
Mr. Chapman stated that he continued to support the concept of 
disclosure by the Advisory Council because it makes recommendations 
which are often adopted by the Board in total but that he was willint 
to narrow the approach to a more reasonable approach of disclosure. 
Mr. Cooper stated that perhaps the Advisory Council sho~d be 
excluded. completely, pointing out that these \'lere people \.,rho 
~'1ere 
volQ~teering their time ~nd it is an unnecessary burden upon them; 
that the Doard was not necessarily persuaded by the opinions of the 
Council since it had the Staff input also; that he was against the 
entire concept. 
On inquiry from Hrs. Koehler, Mr. PmJell stated that when submitting 
the Code to the FPPC, he reVie\'led the background wi th respect to 
the issue of Designated Employee and he read from his letter dated 
February 10, 1977, in i'lhich he advised that the Board expected the 
Commission to review and pass on the matter. 
Mr. POlell pOinted cut 
that BCDC did not include its Citizens Advisory Body in its Code; 
that the San Francisco Art Commission is not included; that the 
opinion of some City Attorneys is that an advisory body should not 
be included; that the Los Angeles City Council has been asked to 
reevaluate their DeSignated Employees; that the FPPC would probably 
hold a hearing on the Code adopted by the Board; that if the motion 
passes he would send another letter to the FPPC with respect to the 
action- taken. 
'On inquiry from Nr. Santana, Hr. Pm'lell stated that· amendments to 
the Cude co~d be made because of chanGed circumstances but that he 
did not feel a hearing needed to be held on the amendments and that 
the Board could at any time take action to amend the Code which would 
be sent to the FPPC for approval. 
Mr. Santana moved a substitute motion to exclude the Advisory Council 
from the Conflict of Interest Code; there fttas no second.

DISCUSSION ON NEH SOURCE REVIEW AND EPA'S SUGGESTED OFF-SET 
POLICY 
Mr. Callaghan revie~'led his report, Emission "Trade -off" or "Off-set 11 ,

He advised that EPA 
~'ras holding a hearing 
in San Francisco on February 23, 1977 ~'li th respect to the matter 
and requested guidance from .the Board for a possible preliminary 
draft on a trade-off re8Ulation. 
Mr. Powell reviet-led his letter dated January 31, 1977 re: EPA 
"Trade-off" Policy. 
On inquiry from Hr. Kenny, Hr. Pot'lell advised that EPA has a 
trade-off policy which is effective in 6 states; that EPA has 
approved a plan with respect to New Source Review for the District 
but not throughout the entire state; that EPA's present requirement 
is that every state ha.ve procedures t'Thereby net-! construction will be 
prohibi ted if it interferes t'li th the attainment or maintenance of 
a.ir quality standards; that EPA may make a change in its basic 
regulation. 
Mr. Cooper stated that it \'las clear that EPA vlill allot-T the District 
to adopt a trade-off policy and he urGed that the District so do, 
pointing out that if this had been in effect when Dow made its applica-
tion they i'lOuld ha.ve been able to build in the District rather than 
. in a foreign cOWltry; tint there was an inherent Wlfairness in allowing 
a plant,alrcady in the area to have a trade-off but not allow a new 
plant to be built; that the adoption of the trade-off policy will 
allOt'l new industry and net';" jobs to locate in the Bay Area. 
He sUGc;estcd the follOt'linc;: (l) ttn. t the 
Bo~rd instruct Staff to Nork vIi th the Advisory Council on preparing 
a draft net'l rule; (2) that the BOD-rd instruct Mr. Chapman to indicate

sections.conclusion: Please be advised that the Fair Political Practices Commission will include Advisory Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code of the Bay Area Pollution Control District only if the members of the Advisory Board satisfy the requirements for being a public official within 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1).

sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District's Advisory Council should be included as 'designated employees' under the District's Conflict of Interest Code, requiring financial disclosure.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC will only include Advisory Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code if they meet the statutory definition of 'public official' under California Administrative Code Section 18700(a)(1).
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is a short advisory response from FPPC Chief Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. The question is inferred from the incoming letter from counsel John F. Powell and supporting documents (Advisory Council report and Board meeting minutes). No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections are explicitly labeled, but the substance is clear from context.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether members of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District's Advisory Council should be included as 'designated employees' under the District's Conflict of Interest Code, requiring financial disclosure.

CONCLUSION: Please be advised that the Fair Political Practices Commission will include Advisory Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code of the Bay Area Pollution Control District only if the members of the Advisory Board satisfy the requirements for being a public official within 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1).

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to a request regarding whether Advisory Council members of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District should be included as 'designated employees' subject to financial disclosure requirements under the District's Conflict of Interest Code. The Commission conditionally stated it would include them only if they satisfy the statutory requirements for being a public official.
